stem: Reflect the point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) over the horizontal line \( y=[[k]] \). What is the \( y \)-coordinate of the image?
Reflect the point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) over the line \( [[a]]x+[[b]]y+([[c]])=0 \). Find the \( x \)-coordinate of the image.
distractor_rationales: This adds the correction term instead of subtracting it.
This uses \( b\) instead of \( a\) in the numerator's leading factor.
This forgets the factor of \( 2\) in the correction term.
This reports the \( y\)-coordinate formula instead of the \( x\)-coordinate formula.
This is off by one from the correct \( x\)-coordinate.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( x\)-coordinate.

stem: Reflect the point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) over the line \( y=[[m]]x \). Find the \( x \)-coordinate of the image.
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the cross term.
This uses \( 1+m^2\) instead of \( 1-m^2\) as the coefficient of \( x_0\).
This reports the \( y\)-coordinate formula instead of the \( x\)-coordinate formula.
This uses a naive slope-multiplication instead of the reflection formula.
This is off by one from the correct \( x\)-coordinate.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( x\)-coordinate.

stem: The point \( P=([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) is reflected over the line \( y=x \) to get \( P' \). Find the distance \( PP' \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of \( \sqrt{2}\) from the diagonal distance.
This uses \( 2\) instead of \( \sqrt{2}\) as the scale factor.
This drops the absolute value, which can go negative.
This adds the coordinates instead of subtracting them.
This is off by one from the correct distance.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct distance.

stem: A circle has center \( ([[h]],[[k]]) \) and radius \( [[r]] \). An external point \( P \) is located \( [[dist]] \) units from the center. Find the length of the tangent segment from \( P \) to the circle.
solution: By the Pythagorean theorem (tangent \( \perp \) radius at the point of tangency), the tangent length is \( \sqrt{d^{2}-r^{2}}=\sqrt{[[dist]]^{2}-[[r]]^{2}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the radius directly instead of using the Pythagorean relationship.
This forgets to square \( d\) and \( r\) before subtracting.
This adds the squares instead of subtracting them.
This reports \( d^2-r^2\) without taking the final square root.
This is off by one from the correct tangent length.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct tangent length.

stem: The point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) lies on the circle centered at \( ([[h]],[[k]]) \) with radius \( [[r]] \). Find the slope of the line tangent to the circle at this point.
solution: The radius to \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) has slope \( \dfrac{[[dy]]}{[[dx]]} \); the tangent line is perpendicular to it, so its slope is \( -\dfrac{[[dx]]}{[[dy]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the required minus sign, using the radius slope itself.
This inverts the ratio before negating.
This inverts the ratio without negating.
This doubles the correct slope.
This is off by one from the correct slope.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct slope.

stem: For what positive value of \( c \) is the line \( y=\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}x+c \) tangent to the circle \( x^{2}+y^{2}=[[r]]^{2} \)?
solution: Tangency requires the distance from the origin to the line to equal the radius: \( \dfrac{|c|}{\sqrt{(p/q)^{2}+1}}=[[r]] \); since \( \sqrt{(p/q)^{2}+1}=\dfrac{[[s]]}{[[q]]} \) (from \( p^{2}+q^{2}=s^{2}\)), the positive solution is \( c=\dfrac{[[r]]\cdot[[s]]}{[[q]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps the roles of \( s\) and \( q\) in the ratio.
This forgets to multiply by the radius \( r\).
This uses \( p\) instead of \( s\) in the numerator.
This reports the radius itself, ignoring the slope's effect on the tangency condition.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( c\).
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct value of \( c\).
